content.text:
Fermat's Little Theorem is one of the most important results in 
elementary number theory.

content.title: Fermat's Little Theorem
content.content:
Let $p$ be prime and let $a$ be an integer with $p \nmid a$. Then
\[
a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}.
\]
Equivalently, for any integer $a$:
\[
a^p \equiv a \pmod{p}.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Consider the $p - 1$ integers $a, 2a, 3a, \ldots, (p-1)a$.

*Claim:* These are congruent, in some order, to $1, 2, 3, \ldots, p-1$ 
modulo $p$.

*Proof of claim:* None of $a, 2a, \ldots, (p-1)a$ is divisible by 
$p$ (since $p \nmid a$ and $p$ is prime). And they are distinct modulo $p$: 
if $ia \equiv ja \pmod{p}$ with $1 \leq i < j \leq p-1$, then 
$p \mid (j-i)a$, so $p \mid (j-i)$, contradicting $0 < j - i < p$.

Therefore, multiplying all the terms:
\[
(a)(2a)(3a) \cdots ((p-1)a) \equiv 1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \cdots (p-1) \pmod{p}.
\]
This gives $a^{p-1} \cdot (p-1)! \equiv (p-1)! \pmod{p}$.

Since $\gcd((p-1)!, p) = 1$, we can cancel to get $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$.

content.title: Fermat's Little Theorem
content.solution:
By Fermat's Little Theorem, $3^{16} \equiv 1 \pmod{17}$.

$100 = 16 \cdot 6 + 4$, so $3^{100} = (3^{16})^6 \cdot 3^4 \equiv 1^6 \cdot 81 \pmod{17}$.

$81 = 4 \cdot 17 + 13$, so $3^{100} \equiv 13 \pmod{17}$.

content.content: If $p$ is prime and $\gcd(a, p) = 1$, then $a^{-1} \equiv a^{p-2} \pmod{p}$.
content.label: Corollary

content.content: By Fermat's Little Theorem, $a \cdot a^{p-2} = a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$.

content.title: Wilson's Theorem
content.content: An integer $n > 1$ is prime if and only if $(n-1)! \equiv -1 \pmod{n}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
$(\Rightarrow)$ Let $p$ be prime. Pair each $a \in \{2, 3, \ldots, p-2\}$ 
with its inverse $a^{-1}$. Since $a \equiv a^{-1}$ iff $a^2 \equiv 1$ iff 
$a \equiv \pm 1$, and we excluded 1 and $p-1$, each element pairs with a 
distinct partner. Thus
\[
2 \cdot 3 \cdots (p-2) \equiv 1 \pmod{p},
\]
so $(p-1)! = 1 \cdot [2 \cdots (p-2)] \cdot (p-1) \equiv 1 \cdot 1 \cdot (-1) = -1 \pmod{p}$.

$(\Leftarrow)$ If $n$ is composite, then $n$ has a factor $d$ with 
$1 < d < n$. Thus $d \mid (n-1)!$, and $d \mid n$, so 
$(n-1)! \not\equiv -1 \pmod{n}$.
